sections.heading: What is Espresso?
sections.content: Espresso is Android's official UI testing framework developed by Google, designed for writing reliable, concise instrumented UI tests that run directly on an Android device or emulator. Unlike unit tests that run on the JVM, Espresso tests run in the context of the actual Android framework — they interact with real Views, Activities, and Fragments exactly as a user would. Espresso's key advantage is its automatic synchronization with the UI thread: it waits for the main thread to be idle and all AsyncTasks to complete before performing any action or assertion, eliminating the need for manual sleep() calls or polling. Espresso tests live in src/androidTest/ and are executed via the Android Test Orchestrator on a connected device or emulator. They are ideal for testing user flows, form validation, navigation, and UI state changes.

sections.heading: Espresso vs Other UI Testing Tools

rows.scope: Single app — white-box testing with access to app internals
rows.speed: Fast for UI tests — automatic synchronization removes explicit waits
rows.best_for: Testing UI flows, View interactions, Activity and Fragment behavior

rows.scope: Cross-app — can interact with system UI, notifications, and other apps
rows.speed: Slower — no automatic synchronization with app thread
rows.best_for: Testing flows that span multiple apps or require system UI interaction

rows.speed: Fast — built-in synchronization with Compose recomposition
rows.best_for: Testing Compose UI components, semantics, and interactions

rows.scope: Single app — simulated Android environment on JVM
rows.speed: Very fast — runs on JVM without device
rows.best_for: Integration tests that need Android framework but not real device rendering

sections.heading: Setting Up Espresso
items: Espresso core: androidTestImplementation("androidx.test.espresso:espresso-core:3.x.x")
AndroidX Test runner: androidTestImplementation("androidx.test:runner:1.x.x")
AndroidX Test rules: androidTestImplementation("androidx.test:rules:1.x.x")
JUnit 4 for instrumented tests: androidTestImplementation("androidx.test.ext:junit:1.x.x")
Espresso intents (for intent verification): androidTestImplementation("androidx.test.espresso:espresso-intents:3.x.x")
Espresso contrib (for RecyclerView, DrawerLayout): androidTestImplementation("androidx.test.espresso:espresso-contrib:3.x.x")
Espresso idling resources: androidTestImplementation("androidx.test.espresso:espresso-idling-resource:3.x.x")
Configure test runner in build.gradle.kts: android { defaultConfig { testInstrumentationRunner = "androidx.test.runner.AndroidJUnitRunner" } }
Disable animations on test device for reliable tests: Developer Options > disable Window/Transition/Animator animation scale, or use AnimationsDisabledRule

sections.heading: Espresso Core Concepts
sections.content: Every Espresso interaction is built from three components working together: ViewMatchers (find the view), ViewActions (perform an action on it), and ViewAssertions (verify its state). The onView() function takes a ViewMatcher to locate a specific view in the current hierarchy, then you chain perform() to interact with it or check() to assert its state. For AdapterViews like ListView, use onData() instead of onView() — it handles scrolling to off-screen items automatically. Espresso's fluent API makes tests read almost like plain English: onView(withId(R.id.submitButton)).perform(click()).check(matches(isEnabled())).

rows.purpose: Locates a view in the current view hierarchy
Performs an interaction on the matched view
Asserts the state or property of the matched view

sections.heading: ViewMatchers — Finding Views
items: withId(R.id.viewId) — matches a view by its resource ID — most common and reliable matcher
withText("Hello") — matches a view by its exact text content
withText(containsString("Hell")) — matches a view whose text contains the given substring
withHint("Enter email") — matches an EditText by its hint text
withContentDescription("Send") — matches a view by its content description — useful for icon buttons
isDisplayed() — matches only views that are visible on screen
isEnabled() — matches views that are currently enabled
isChecked() / isNotChecked() — matches CheckBox, RadioButton, or Switch state
hasFocus() — matches the view that currently has keyboard focus
withParent(withId(R.id.toolbar)) — matches a view that is a direct child of the specified parent
allOf(withId(R.id.button), isDisplayed()) — combines multiple matchers with logical AND
anyOf(withText("OK"), withText("Confirm")) — matches if any of the matchers match

rows.description: Performs a single tap on the view
Performs a double tap on the view
Performs a long press on the view
Types the given text into an EditText — clicks the view first
Replaces the entire text content of an EditText without clicking first
Clears all text from an EditText
Simulates pressing the system Back button
Presses the IME action button on the keyboard (e.g. Done, Search, Next)
Scrolls a ScrollView until the matched view is visible on screen
Swipes left or right on the view — useful for ViewPager navigation
Dismisses the soft keyboard — call after typing to avoid obscuring views

sections.heading: ViewAssertions — Verifying Views
items: check(matches(isDisplayed())) — asserts the view is visible on screen
check(matches(not(isDisplayed()))) — asserts the view is not visible
check(matches(withText("Expected text"))) — asserts the view displays specific text
check(matches(isEnabled())) — asserts the view is enabled and interactive
check(matches(not(isEnabled()))) — asserts the view is disabled
check(matches(isChecked())) — asserts a CheckBox or Switch is checked
check(matches(hasErrorText("Required field"))) — asserts an EditText shows a specific error message
check(doesNotExist()) — asserts the view does not exist in the hierarchy at all
check(matches(withEffectiveVisibility(Visibility.GONE))) — asserts view has GONE visibility
check(matches(withContentDescription("Profile photo"))) — asserts the content description matches

sections.heading: ActivityScenario and Test Rules
sections.content: Modern Espresso tests use ActivityScenario (from AndroidX Test) to launch and control Activities, replacing the older ActivityTestRule. ActivityScenario provides a clean lifecycle-aware API for launching an Activity, interacting with it on the main thread, and moving it through lifecycle states. The ActivityScenarioRule JUnit rule launches the Activity before each test and closes it after automatically. For Fragment testing, FragmentScenario allows launching individual Fragments in isolation without needing the full Activity, enabling more focused Fragment-level UI tests.

sections.heading: ActivityScenario Usage
items: Launch with rule: @get:Rule val activityRule = ActivityScenarioRule(MainActivity::class.java)
Launch manually: val scenario = ActivityScenario.launch(MainActivity::class.java)
Launch with Intent: val scenario = ActivityScenario.launch<DetailActivity>(Intent(context, DetailActivity::class.java).putExtra("id", 123))
Run code on main thread: scenario.onActivity { activity -> activity.someMethod() }
Move to lifecycle state: scenario.moveToState(Lifecycle.State.STARTED)
Recreate Activity (simulate rotation): scenario.recreate()
Get Activity result: scenario.result — returns ActivityResult with resultCode and data
Always close scenario: scenario.close() — or use ActivityScenarioRule which closes automatically
Fragment testing: val scenario = launchFragmentInContainer<HomeFragment>(themeResId = R.style.Theme_App)

rows.description: Scrolls the RecyclerView to the specified adapter position
Scrolls until an item matching the given ViewMatcher is visible
Performs a ViewAction on the item at the given adapter position
Scrolls to and performs an action on the first item matching the matcher
Performs an action on a ViewHolder matching the given ViewHolder matcher

sections.heading: Testing Intents with Espresso Intents
items: Use Intents.init() before the test and Intents.release() after — or use IntentsRule which handles this automatically
IntentsRule: @get:Rule val intentsRule = IntentsRule() — initializes and releases Intents around each test
Verify an Intent was sent: intended(hasComponent(DetailActivity::class.java.name))
Verify Intent extras: intended(hasExtra("userId", 42))
Verify implicit Intent: intended(hasAction(Intent.ACTION_SEND))
Stub an outgoing Intent result: intending(hasAction(Intent.ACTION_PICK)).respondWith(ActivityResult(Activity.RESULT_OK, resultData))
Stubbing prevents real external apps from launching during tests — essential for testing camera, gallery, or file picker flows
Combine matchers: intended(allOf(hasComponent(DetailActivity::class.java.name), hasExtra("id", 123)))

rows.description: An IdlingResource tells Espresso when your app is busy with asynchronous work — Espresso waits until all registered resources are idle before proceeding
Increments a counter when work starts and decrements when it finishes — idle when counter is zero
rows.example: val idlingResource = CountingIdlingResource("NetworkCalls"); idlingResource.increment() before call; idlingResource.decrement() in callback

rows.description: Built-in idling resource for OkHttp network calls — automatically tracks in-flight network requests
Register in @Before and unregister in @After to avoid leaking between tests
Use when your app uses non-standard async mechanisms not automatically detected by Espresso — coroutines, RxJava, custom thread pools, Retrofit callbacks
rows.example: Espresso automatically handles AsyncTask and Handler — custom coroutine dispatchers need IdlingResources or should be replaced with test dispatchers

sections.heading: Writing a Complete Espresso Test
items: Annotate the test class: @RunWith(AndroidJUnit4::class)
Declare ActivityScenarioRule: @get:Rule val activityRule = ActivityScenarioRule(LoginActivity::class.java)
Write the test method annotated with @Test
Use onView(withId(R.id.emailEditText)).perform(typeText("user@example.com"), closeSoftKeyboard())
Use onView(withId(R.id.passwordEditText)).perform(typeText("password123"), closeSoftKeyboard())
Use onView(withId(R.id.loginButton)).perform(click())
Assert navigation result: onView(withId(R.id.homeTitle)).check(matches(isDisplayed()))
Use @Before to set up test data or mock dependencies via Hilt test modules
Use @After to clean up database state or reset shared preferences between tests

rows.fix: Disable Window, Transition, and Animator animation scales in Developer Options or use AnimationsDisabledRule — animations cause Espresso to time out waiting for the UI to settle
Register an IdlingResource for async operations — Thread.sleep() makes tests slow and flaky; Espresso's synchronization is designed to eliminate explicit waits

rows.mistake: Matching views by text that changes with locale
rows.fix: Match by resource ID (withId()) or content description instead of hardcoded text strings — text changes with localization

rows.mistake: Not handling the soft keyboard obscuring views
rows.fix: Call closeSoftKeyboard() after typeText() actions before performing click() on views that may be hidden behind the keyboard
Use RecyclerViewActions from espresso-contrib for RecyclerView interactions — onView() cannot scroll to off-screen RecyclerView items

rows.mistake: Tests depending on each other or device state
rows.fix: Each test must set up its own data and clean up after itself — use @Before and @After; never rely on a previous test having run

rows.mistake: Not using ActivityScenario for Activity lifecycle testing
rows.fix: Use ActivityScenario.recreate() to test configuration changes; use moveToState() to test lifecycle transitions rather than relying on device rotation

sections.heading: Running and Organizing Espresso Tests
items: Run all instrumented tests: ./gradlew connectedAndroidTest — requires a connected device or running emulator
Run a specific test class: ./gradlew connectedAndroidTest -Pandroid.testInstrumentationRunnerArguments.class=com.example.LoginTest
Run a specific test method: append #methodName to the class argument
Use Android Test Orchestrator to run each test in its own process — prevents test pollution: testInstrumentationRunner = "androidx.test.orchestrator.AndroidTestOrchestrator"
Organize tests by feature in packages mirroring the main source set: androidTest/java/com/example/login/LoginTest.kt
Use test sharding on CI to run tests in parallel across multiple emulators with Firebase Test Lab or Gradle managed devices
Generate test reports: build/reports/androidTests/connected/index.html — HTML report with pass/fail per test
Take screenshots on test failure using FailureHandler or Screenshot.capture() from AndroidX Test for easier debugging

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Espresso is the standard framework for Android UI testing, providing automatic UI thread synchronization, a fluent three-part API of ViewMatchers, ViewActions, and ViewAssertions, and deep integration with the Android testing ecosystem. Use ActivityScenarioRule to launch and manage Activities, RecyclerViewActions for list interactions, and Espresso Intents for verifying and stubbing outgoing Intents. Register IdlingResources for custom asynchronous operations that Espresso cannot detect automatically. Always disable animations on test devices, avoid Thread.sleep(), match views by ID rather than hardcoded text, and ensure each test is fully independent. Run instrumented tests with ./gradlew connectedAndroidTest and use the Android Test Orchestrator on CI for isolated, reliable test execution.
